speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING] [SPEAKING GERMAN] [LAUGHING] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 4, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Kraus. My name is Jill Greenberg. We're in Brookline, Massachusetts, in the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The language being used is English. And this is tape number 7. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked me what my dreams or plans were in this rehabilitation camp. Well, it wasn't a camp. It was more a castle, a large villa called [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was difficult to imagine [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] having a normal life without parents. This was a new phase of my life to reconcile myself to the fact that I'm alone. And it took many, many years to make peace with that realization. Just as it was very hard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to accept that the life that I was used to living will never return, that the pre-war era is gone forever. I think the first thoughts, although not jubilations of the end of the war and the liberation, was that now this is over, and life will return to normal. And so this next phase was to learn that it will never be so-called normal. And a-- a 15-year-old could not yet imagine a different life or a better life or a equal life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was seeking to-- to adjust, simply. And that did not happen overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at [INAUDIBLE]-- and as you indicated, you were treated very well. Were there any people there to talk with you about your experience or try to help you deal with some of what you had experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe so. I think that this was-- there were no trained social workers or psychiatrists. These were all volunteers or people who were trying to do the best for orphans. And it's interesting because now, when I use the word 'orphan' and translate it into Czech, which is [CZECH] I don't think that I've ever used that in my life, that I'm an orphan. So that even shows you more that I was trying to ignore the fact and not make it acceptable as-- as even being labeled such. The-- the director of this institution, whose name is [? Seamus ?] [? Lepeter, ?] was posthumously awarded one of those Righteous People in Jerusalem. I did not know that at that time. I mean, he was just the wonderful organizer and volunteered to help the children and must have done a fabulous job to requisition all of these villas and castles to accommodate children. And it's possible that these were not just Czech children, that they were children from displaced persons and other nationalities, and refugees. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started corresponding with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Vera Lowenbach. She was about 10 or 15 years younger than my mother. They were together in Stutthof. And she's the one who then was the witness that my mother died. Even though she didn't literally see her die, she knows of her death.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she contacted me. She must have found, from these refugee centers, that I came back and where I was sent. And so she sought me out. And I went to visit her. And we jointly decided that I would stay with her. She had her nephew there and another niece. She has lost two small children and a husband. And her loss [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is something that she has never lived down to this day. She's now close to 85. And we are still in close contact because she's the last link, for me, to my parents and their suffering, the last witness for me, last memory. Everybody else is from my generation that is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was living on her parents' villa in a small town 15 kilometers from Nachod, where I was-- where I lived. And she had these three other survivors there that were her relatives. And she was nursing us back to life. She was liberated a little earlier. She was also in Auschwitz and other camps. Her children were sent to the gas chamber the same day my father perished. And her husband was killed in a-- air-- by-- by a bomb, by a British or American plane in-- in another concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She took care of us, fed us, lodged us. And she got a tutor for me because I haven't been to school for four years and I had a lot to catch up, and made it possible for me to take some exams and organize my acceptance to the high school, gymnasium in Nachod. Since [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was-- her-- her house where she then lived was a-- a distance away from the school, we then agreed that during the week I would live with another couple, both of whom were survivors. That was Rudolf and Wilma Beck, the person that I met in Auschwitz that I mentioned earlier. They lost their son. And they were very interested in having me come and live with them and help me through these difficult times. And, at first, I stayed there during the week, and Saturday afternoon, bicycled to Vera. And then Sunday evening or Monday morning, I would bicycle back into town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually, Vera [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] married another survivor, a doctor who lived in another town, and-- and moved away so that I lived permanently with the Becks in my hometown. And that worked very well. They were very sensitive and understanding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a great deal to catch up and to learn in school, and struggled to keep my grades. I did lose one school year. I didn't go immediately to the grade I belonged for my age. But I did go to school there for three years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle, who spent the war in Palestine with his family, returned to Nachod and insisted that I stay with him. He just felt that he respected, whether I liked it or not, it was his duty to his sister, deceased sister, that he takes care of me. So very reluctantly, I moved to their house. They had a son two years younger than I am. We did not get along very well because there was a lack of just understanding the circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was not altogether happy. So when they decided that things are getting bad again, that the Communists were gaining power and that the future was not bright for them-- he was an owner of a textile mill. And they were giving him difficulty to return it. He decided to emigrate again with his family. And when they did, I moved back to the Becks. To their credit, I have to say that they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all conspired, so to speak, to ship me out also. They felt that the future was not in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'They' being the Becks? Or 'they' being your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both, both families. And my aunt, who was a great entrepreneur, did a lot of running around to get papers for me. And then the Becks finished the job to get permission that I leave the country. And this effort was redoubled in February of 1948 when the Communists took over the government in the Putsch and ended the three years of democracy that the republic enjoyed. So then it was getting more and more difficult to get out of the country. But as a orphan, the American Joint Distribution offered to pay for my transportation and arrange for taking care of me in Canada. I was unable to get a visa to the United States because the Americans simply did not want to be flooded with immigrants from Europe and had very strict national quotas. And the Czech quota was well oversubscribed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to say goodbye to my hometown, which I was very accustomed to. And I had many friends in school. And reluctantly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went first to England, where I had a cousin, and then by boat to Halifax, Canada, with other refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the refugees there was one of the group. His name was Pavel Kummerman. And we were-- the last few years, we are looking for him everywhere but couldn't find him. But another boy whom I didn't even know-- we didn't know each other. But I had a photograph taken on the ship. And two years ago, he looked at that photographs and said, that's me, and this is Pavel Kummerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, 50 years later we found each other again, but not the third one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to be going to Canada? Why were you going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Joint. That-- there was only a-- one could go to Australia or to Canada. Those were the countries that-- and it turned out, later on, that the reason I could get to Canada is that the Canadians opened a-- were willing to issue visas when the war started to-- to orphans in Central Europe, Jews that were fleeing Hitler. But the powers that were administering the law were very antisemitic in Canada. And they just dragged their feet, just like many of the American consulates who-- who did not wish to endorse the visas that were issued by the State Department. There are many horror stories like that. These visas were never used to go to Canada. So in 1948, we could get into Canada on those-- on that quota. And when I came to Canada, there were many places where Jews were not allowed to buy or rent property. And McGill had numerous classes in the Medical Faculty. So it-- it was not a-- a Jew-friendly country by any means.

speaker: interviewer
text: By 'numerous classes,' you mean a limited number of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They set a quota how many Jews could enter the Medical Faculty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- who did you live with when you got to Canada, and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Joint arranged for all of this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- I think we had some say where we wanted to go. And I opted for Montreal. But some went to Saskatchewan. Some went to Toronto. It was a eye-opener to land in Halifax. And it's-- somehow, I see it in my mind, being on the boat and seeing the cliffs and the-- the land and the little wooden houses. And the first impression was, well, this is a summer colony.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Czechoslovakia, wooden houses were either on the farm-- but even those were stucco. But there were summer colonies. And the-- the second thing was that suddenly were all these big cars that we saw in movies and we thought, well, those were just for-- for movies, you know, stage, set. And here everybody was driving these dinosaurs.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The third surprise was that we were taken to various Jewish homes overnight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They sort of parcelled us out. And they gave us ginger and Coca-Cola. And we've never had that in our lives. So that-- I recall these little things as being great surprises. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] However, years later, we made a trip to Halifax. And I couldn't recognize anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was so changed in the 45 years. The next day, they put us on a train to Montreal, which was a long journey. And there we stayed in the Jewish Center. They had kind of like a YMHA, or what is it called? Young Men's Hebrew Association. And they were slowly, with the help of social workers, taking care of us individually. Some were sent into other cities. Some were offered for adoption, those who are willing to do that. And I was put in a home of a Romanian family that the Joint paid a monthly fee for feeding me and housing me. It was a simple family. He was a garment worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it was an eye-opener for me because I don't come from a home that was observant nor kosher. And this was a observant home, or pseudo-observant home, but of much lower circumstances that I was used to as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- was there-- how did that feel to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was strange. I mean, I felt very constrained. And it-- it was hard to adapt. And somehow, if I had a dream, it was that I will become an architect. And I will sort of-- this was not a defined dream, but just the feeling that I have to go back to the life that I considered appropriate or that I was brought up to. And I was lucky because I was the-- one of the few of those orphaned refugees who was then able to go to the university. Most of them went to trade schools or straight to work or into business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen that you-- you were able to go to the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just couldn't see any other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --future for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to apply? Did you have to get scholarships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the above.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to high school, my last year of high school. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't unplug. Can you go-- no.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a fairly Jewish high school called [INAUDIBLE]. It was in a section that was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] primarily by Jewish occupants, Jewish residents. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I made friends with some of the boys. This was the last school-- last class of high school. And I had in one year to learn English. When I first came to school, I didn't know whether they were teaching chemistry or Shakespeare.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet I thought that I had learned some English [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over the years. Because in 1947, I was sent to England to a very rich second cousin who paid for me to go into a fancy boarding school for the summer. It was ironic-- I'm just going back now-- that in 1947, England was much worse off--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --than Czechoslovakia. They still had food rationing and-- and card-- you know, food-- ration cards. Whereas we had everything we wanted. So here was the victorious British Empire that was still on its knees two and a half years after the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you-- why did you happen to choose Montreal instead of Saskatchewan or any of these other places? Did you have a sense of what each was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew some of-- my guardian identified a number of families from Czechoslovakia who knew my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And secondly, I was hoping that eventually I would be able to enter the United States. I had a cousin in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, that cousin came three days or four days after I arrived in Montreal and took me to the American consulate and registered me for-- for-- applied for entering the United States. And I had to wait almost three years before my quota came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to decide on becoming an architect?

speaker: interviewee
text: By elimination. Obviously, the medical profession would have been preferred. But I was very scary of even talking about injuries or medicine. And the main decisive point was that I had so much to catch up that I chose [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a high school that did not have Latin, because I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --missed three years of Latin. And in those days, you had to have Latin for most classical studies, including medicine. So I would only be fit for an engineer without Latin. And engineering, as such, didn't appeal to me. And for some reason, I thought that architecture-- even though I knew nothing about architecture nor architects. It sort of was a mythical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prejudice, rather than choice. And so I wanted to-- after I finished high school, which was quite a difficult year-- especially my appearance was so outlandishly difficult--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --different from the young Canadians, just the type of clothes that I brought with me in a big leather suitcase. You know, was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that hard for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very. And I didn't like Canada. I didn't like the family I lived in-- with. I didn't like the Eastern-type Jewish people. I didn't like the prejudices of the Canadians against the Jews. And I wanted to go back to Czechoslovakia. And but I promised that I will stick it out for a year. And when the year was over, it was pretty clear that the Communists were tightening their grip and that going back would have been a worse choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you finished at McGill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was one year of high school. Then I applied to McGill. And I was accepted into architecture. Our first two years were mostly pre-architecture, which was mostly engineering and catching up the deficiencies of a quasi-American high school. We were lectured on at length. And we don't know the English language properly, nor any math or chemistry. So they were feeding us all of this to catch up. In fact, the first year, because of the tremendous influx of veterans that could go to universities, we were shipped to a-- outside Montreal to an old air force base to-- to take classes, which was somewhat reminiscent of the army barracks. Well, they were army barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then your visa finally came through.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went two years. Then the next year, I moved back to Montreal. And I lived with some very kind people who just were interested to helping Jewish refugees. They were a Jewish family, very cultured. He was a lawyer. And she was a musician. And they were very nice to me. And it happened to be on the same street that my Czech friends lived. So I had some advantage there. And I was very much absorbed with learning, with studying. I was very enthusiastic about my studies. And that's what kept me going and thinking about other things. And I was not at all in touch with any refugees or any contemporary-- you know, that group of boys. Didn't know of any. And so my thoughts were mostly on my studies. And after almost three years, the visa came through, just in the middle of the summer. So I decided to have a summer job first in British Colombia. And then, just before the year started, I went to the States without being assured that I would be accepted to university. But fortunately, the day I crossed the border at Rouses Point, I got a telegram from Columbia that they had an opening and accepted me. So, again, that was a question of good luck, because I didn't-- didn't apply to any other place. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived in New York, and you went to Colombia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must admit that I disliked New York from the day I arrived there. My cousin came in a Lincoln Continental--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --to pick me up at Grand Central Station. And they were trying to show me all the skyscrapers and down Fifth Avenue. And I was totally unimpressed [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and probably remained skeptical ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. And after you finished your studies there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at Columbia for four years. And-- and at that time, they only awarded bachelor of architecture. And I went, immediately got a job. And for two years, I worked very hard to save enough money to take a grand tour of Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I became a American citizen in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1956. And the next year, I bought myself a one-way ticket on a steamer. And when-- at midnight of September 1, when the rates got cheaper for-- for such trips, I sailed for Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]
